title: Hostages

jokes.text: I'd be terrible in a hostage situation, even as a hostage taker.
jokes.setup: Selina is watching news coverage of a hostage crisis and expressing sympathy for the victims.
jokes.punchline: She pivots immediately from empathy to imagining herself as the villain — and finding herself wanting on that side too.
jokes.explanation: A cold-open gem. The misdirection is elegant — starts with sympathy, pivots to self-absorption, then undercuts even that. The 'even as a hostage taker' addition is the key: it's not just narcissism, it's incompetent narcissism. Deeply character-specific to Selina.

jokes.text: he knows I've got a bigger role in the White House now, which means I've got a bigger dick, which means he can suck it.
jokes.setup: Selina explains why Maddox hasn't responded to her invitation — framing political power in anatomical terms.
jokes.punchline: The escalating anatomical metaphor ('bigger role → bigger dick → suck it') delivered with breezy delight.
jokes.explanation: Classic Veep profanity-as-power-metaphor. Works because it's delivered with genuine glee, and the logical chain (role → dick → suck it) has a pleasing internal structure. Not exceptional but very functional.

jokes.text: That's gonna electrify DC. — Oh, it's gonna AC DC.
jokes.setup: Amy predicts the word change will 'electrify DC.'
jokes.punchline: Dan riffs immediately with 'AC DC' — both an electricity pun and a rock band name.
jokes.explanation: A quick, competent pun. The AC/DC double meaning works in two steps (electrical current + band) and is delivered with good timing, but it's a low-stakes wordplay moment with no particular character specificity. Functional joke filler.

jokes.text: Robust is a good word. And you don't hear it often, like bumptious.
jokes.setup: Selina continues praising 'robust' as a word.
jokes.punchline: She compares it to 'bumptious' as another underused word — a completely bizarre and obscure comparison that reveals her verbal self-satisfaction.
jokes.explanation: 'Bumptious' as a comparator is a perfectly absurd choice — it sounds vaguely obscene and is rarely used, perfectly illustrating Selina's self-important relationship with language. The specificity makes it funny.

jokes.text: Yeah, it's what you want to hear in a hostage crisis. Robust, not bumptious.
jokes.setup: Selina rhapsodizes about 'robust' and 'bumptious' as good words.
jokes.punchline: Dan drily distinguishes which one belongs in a hostage crisis as if this is a serious policy distinction.
jokes.explanation: A tight deadpan line. The logic is funny — yes, 'bumptious' would be wrong in a crisis — delivered as if this is a real editorial distinction. Economy is excellent: four words do all the work.

jokes.text: Robust? I like it. It makes me think of wine. It's actually how I like my reds.
jokes.setup: Selina asks Mike's opinion on the word 'robust' as a political signal.
jokes.punchline: Mike responds not with political analysis but with his wine preferences.
jokes.explanation: Perfect Mike characterization — asked for political spin insight, he immediately defaults to food/drink appreciation. His obliviousness is played completely straight, which makes it funnier.

jokes.text: You like your reds through a bendy straw, too, right, Mike?
jokes.setup: Mike proudly declares his wine preferences after contributing nothing useful.
jokes.punchline: Dan mocks Mike's sophistication by implying he drinks wine like a child drinks juice.
jokes.explanation: A crisp Dan insult — 'bendy straw' is a specific and pleasingly childish image. It doesn't just call Mike dumb; it paints a visual. The specificity elevates it above a generic putdown.

jokes.text: You got donut on you, Mike.
jokes.setup: Dan has just delivered a sharp insult to Mike.
jokes.punchline: He immediately pivots to pointing out Mike literally has donut residue on himself — a capper that confirms Mike's schlubby image.
jokes.explanation: Four words doing double duty: it's both a literal observation AND a metaphorical insult (Mike is slovenly/childish). The non sequitur landing after the straw line gives it comic rhythm.

jokes.text: VP stands for 'very precious.' Okay?
jokes.setup: Gary is briefing the substitute aide Cliff on how to care for Selina.
jokes.punchline: Gary's sincere, protective instinct towards Selina expressed as a ridiculous acronym reinterpretation.
jokes.explanation: Deeply Gary. His pathological devotion to Selina compressed into an absurd acronym he clearly invented himself and is very proud of. The 'Okay?' with presumably zero self-awareness is perfect character work.

jokes.text: This is the Veeplopedia. It's everything you'll ever need to know. Wow. Hope it comes with CliffsNotes.
jokes.setup: Gary presents Cliff with a thick binder of information about how to serve Selina, called 'the Veeplopedia.'
jokes.punchline: Cliff puns on his own name — CliffsNotes — while implying the Veeplopedia is dauntingly large.
jokes.explanation: A solid pun — 'CliffsNotes' lands on both the product and the character's name, with the added implication that even the cliff-notes version of Gary's obsessive binder would be overwhelming. Sets up the next joke well.

jokes.text: That was a play on my name. My name is Cliff. Don't do jokes, Cliff. I don't have a sense of humor and neither should you.
jokes.setup: Cliff makes the CliffsNotes pun, clearly pleased with himself.
jokes.punchline: Gary explains the joke back to Cliff (as if he didn't get it), then sternly prohibits humor in Selina's service — delivered with total sincerity.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The joke-within-a-joke structure is airtight: Gary explains the pun as if Cliff is an idiot, then delivers the actual laugh with his profoundly humorless worldview — 'I don't have a sense of humor and neither should you.' This is total character: Gary's devotion to Selina has purged him of all levity. The meta quality (a character explaining then banning jokes) is also genuinely original. Justifies the 8+ score.

jokes.text: How do you make two perfectly pleasant words so irritating?
jokes.setup: Ben greets Selina with 'Good morning.'
jokes.punchline: Selina responds with a precise, contemptuous observation about Ben's delivery.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful compressed insult. The phrasing is precise — she acknowledges the words themselves are fine, the problem is him. This is the Veep style at its best: maximum contempt in minimum syllables. The elegance of targeting delivery rather than content is original.

jokes.text: And here he is, the man who can't take a leak without polling his balls.
jokes.setup: Jonah enters with Kent's polling data.
jokes.punchline: Selina mocks Kent's obsession with polling by suggesting he applies it even to his own anatomy before urinating.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best insults. The joke fuses two things perfectly: the Veep universe's obsession with polling, and profanity. 'Polling his balls' is not just crude — it's a logically extended metaphor that only works in this political context. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: He had a pro-strike hawk boner the size of Jonah.
jokes.setup: Ben describes POTUS's hawkish attitude the previous night about the hostage situation.
jokes.punchline: His military enthusiasm is measured in units of 'Jonah' — treating Jonah as a standard unit of absurd scale.
jokes.explanation: Jonah as a unit of measurement for something simultaneously impressive and ridiculous is a recurring Veep joke that works because Jonah is both annoyingly tall and completely useless. 'Hawk boner' as political jargon is also a good coinage.

jokes.text: There's cultures that don't even have any numbers and they do just fine.
jokes.setup: Kent presents polling data and cold numbers. Ben says he hates numbers.
jokes.punchline: Ben doubles down with a pseudo-anthropological defense of innumeracy.
jokes.explanation: Ben's brand of exhausted, cynical bluster is perfectly encapsulated here. He doesn't just hate numbers — he has constructed a whole cultural-anthropological framework to justify it. The 'and they do just fine' is the key deadpan finisher.

jokes.text: Well, Ben, it seems like you're ill-suited for your job and you should resign.
jokes.setup: Ben has just complained about numbers in a data-driven White House.
jokes.punchline: Kent responds with complete logical precision — if you hate numbers, you should quit — delivered without a hint of warmth.
jokes.explanation: Quintessential Kent — no social grace, just pure logical conclusion delivered with the deadpan of a man who genuinely believes resigning is the obvious solution. Character integration is high because this only works coming from Kent.

jokes.text: Yeah, precise and surgical, like your lobotomy, Kent.
jokes.setup: Selina agrees with calls for a precise, surgical military strike.
jokes.punchline: She uses 'surgical' as a springboard to insult Kent's emotionlessness, implying he had a lobotomy.
jokes.explanation: Clean Veep insult — the wordplay between 'surgical strike' and 'surgical lobotomy' is economical, and the target (Kent's robot-like demeanor) is on-brand. Selina gets to agree AND insult in one breath.

jokes.text: No, that's cherry red. — Cerise. — I'm sorry, sir. I must have a very specific form of color blindness.
jokes.setup: Kent asks for his cerise-colored binder. Jonah brings cherry red. Kent corrects him.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's desperate, self-deprecating response — diagnosing himself with 'a very specific form of color blindness' rather than admitting he just didn't know what cerise was.
jokes.explanation: Jonah's sycophantic self-diagnosis rather than just admitting ignorance is perfect character work. The specificity — 'a very specific form' — is the funny part, because it reveals he's trying to medicalize his own incompetence.

jokes.text: I cook these noodles every day... Oh, the noodle analogy. You guys are gonna love this.
jokes.setup: Kent begins an extended food analogy to explain his polling strategy.
jokes.punchline: Ben sarcastically pre-hypes the analogy ('you guys are gonna love this') while clearly dreading it — implying this is a known, dreaded recurring behavior from Kent.
jokes.explanation: The joke is Ben's withering 'you guys are gonna love this' — it signals to the audience that we're about to endure something insufferable, AND it retroactively implies Kent does this often. Economical setup for the extended noodle bit.

jokes.text: I will leave.
jokes.setup: Kent begins the noodle analogy with a pompous preamble.
jokes.punchline: Ben flatly announces he will leave — not as a threat, just a statement of intent — before Kent has even gotten to the analogy.
jokes.explanation: Three words. Two of which are 'I will.' The absolute economy is perfect — Ben's contempt for Kent's analogies is delivered as a matter-of-fact statement rather than an outburst. It's funnier that he doesn't leave, too. Perfect Ben moment.

jokes.text: Any less and they will be flaccid and damp like a lady's hair in the rain.
jokes.setup: Kent explains the precise microwave timing for his noodles as an analogy for hostage strategy.
jokes.punchline: His clinical description of undercooked noodles as 'flaccid and damp like a lady's hair in the rain' is bizarre and specific to the point of absurdity.
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely original. The simile is unexpected ('lady's hair in the rain') and weirdly specific in a way that reveals Kent's alien mind — he has compared noodles and hair with complete sincerity. The word 'flaccid' applied to noodles is also funnier than it has any right to be. Earns its 8+ for genuine weirdness.

jokes.text: Why are we talking about noodles? — No, let me rephrase that. Why the fuck are we talking about noodles?
jokes.setup: Kent is mid-noodle-analogy in the middle of a hostage crisis.
jokes.punchline: Ben's self-correction — upgrading his objection from polite to profane — is the joke; the rephrase adds nothing except an expletive, which is funnier than anything he could have added.
jokes.explanation: The structure is the joke: the 'let me rephrase' sets up an expectation of a more articulate objection, but the only change is 'the fuck.' This is Veep's profanity-as-emphasis at its most precise. The self-correction format is also slightly meta.

jokes.text: You just timed out that whole shit analogy for your little ding? That is fucking pathetic.
jokes.setup: Kent concludes his elaborate noodle analogy precisely as a microwave bell dings — it becomes clear he timed the entire speech to the microwave.
jokes.punchline: Ben's furious reaction reveals that Kent orchestrated the whole thing as performance art for a ding.
jokes.explanation: The episode's best payoff. The entire noodle sequence has been building to this: the revelation that Kent timed his entire hostage-crisis analogy to match the microwave precisely is both absurd and entirely believable for his character. Ben's 'fucking pathetic' is the perfect capper — it's Ben's own impotent fury that rounds out the joke. The setup is long, the payoff is proportional, and the structural integrity is exceptional. Justifies 8.5+ craft.

jokes.text: Probably got his head stuck in her ass.
jokes.setup: Selina notes with puzzlement that Maddox hasn't responded, calling it 'eerie.' Gary agrees.
jokes.punchline: Then Selina (or Gary — contextually Gary given the next line of agreement) suggests Maddox is too far up someone's ass to respond.
jokes.explanation: A functional piece of profane political snark but with no particular originality. The 'head stuck in ass' construction is common enough that it reads as filler. Decent rhythm in context but not memorable.

jokes.text: They don't salute indoors, Gary. — They don't salute indoors, Gary. (both simultaneously)
jokes.setup: Gary notices Marines aren't saluting Selina and is bothered on her behalf.
jokes.punchline: Selina corrects Gary — and the joke is that she delivers the correction in the exact same condescending tone Gary was using, implying both are wrong to think this matters.
jokes.explanation: The simultaneous delivery — both saying the same thing in the same corrective tone at the same moment — is a visual and audio gag that works in performance. It reveals that Selina and Gary have fused to the point that they echo each other, which is both funny and slightly disturbing.

jokes.text: I was married to a Devil Dog, but he wasn't a Marine. He was more of a devil.
jokes.setup: Briefed that Marines are called 'Devil Dogs,' Selina tries to connect with the troops.
jokes.punchline: She pivots to her ex-husband Andrew being 'more of a devil' — a self-deprecating marital admission in front of a military audience.
jokes.explanation: A nicely structured two-step: sets up via the Devil Dogs briefing, lands on a self-aware marital quip. The Marines' polite chuckling ('She's so funny') adds a cringe layer — they're humoring their superior. The Andrew reference gives it character depth.

jokes.text: She's so funny. (Marine, clearly just being polite)
jokes.setup: Selina delivers her 'devil' pun to the assembled Marines.
jokes.punchline: The Marine's dutiful 'She's so funny' — clearly a polite, possibly bewildered reaction — underscores the awkward gap between Selina's performance and its reception.
jokes.explanation: The cringe layer on top of Selina's joke: the Marine's response is technically a compliment but lands as damning with faint praise. Works best as a beat within the larger 'Selina trying to charm the troops' sequence.

jokes.text: Small arms means guns, ma'am. — Oh, really, Gary? Not T-rex hands?
jokes.setup: A Marine explains that the 'small arms' have a range of 25 yards. Gary helpfully clarifies that 'small arms means guns, ma'am.'
jokes.punchline: Selina's withering 'Not T-rex hands?' — she's insulted by Gary's condescension and expresses it with a specific, absurd image.
jokes.explanation: A standout line. 'T-rex hands' is a specific, weird, and funny image — and the fact that Selina's insult toward Gary is so precise and so strange is exactly the kind of originality Veep excels at. The rhythm of the setup/response is crisp. Justifies high scores — it's the kind of throwaway that gets quoted.

jokes.text: In anger, too, I might add.
jokes.setup: Maddox says he's fired a gun before, declining to try the range.
jokes.punchline: He adds 'in anger, too, I might add' — a casually alarming detail delivered with total nonchalance.
jokes.explanation: The economy is exceptional — six words, massive reveal. Maddox casually dropping that he's shot a gun in anger (at a person?) as a biographical footnote is both menacing and darkly funny. The self-satisfied 'I might add' is perfect character framing.

jokes.text: What the fuck? — This could backfire. — It's not gonna backfire. The gun could literally backfire. That's where the phrase comes from.
jokes.setup: Gary warns Selina that shooting the gun 'could backfire' politically.
jokes.punchline: Selina notes that the gun could LITERALLY backfire, reclaiming the idiom's original meaning in a way that makes Gary look like an idiot.
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed etymological joke — reclaiming a metaphor's literal meaning in a situation where that meaning is also relevant. The 'That's where the phrase comes from' is particularly good: delivered as a corrective factual statement, it's funnier than any insult.

jokes.text: Ma'am, this could really chap Maddox's ass, too. — Oh, yeah, it would, wouldn't it?
jokes.setup: Amy frames shooting the gun in terms of its political effect on Maddox.
jokes.punchline: Selina's dawning delight — the political calculation is doing the opposite of deterring her; it's motivating her.
jokes.explanation: A good beat but more character moment than joke. The humor is in Selina's childish glee at annoying Maddox. Functional character comedy that moves the story while getting a chuckle.

jokes.text: You could chip a veneer and I don't really want that to happen. — I don't care. I'm going in.
jokes.setup: Gary tries to talk Selina out of shooting by warning about her dental work.
jokes.punchline: Gary's most pressing concern about a live weapons exercise is her dental veneer; Selina dismisses it with mock military resolve.
jokes.explanation: Gary's priorities are perfect here: not her safety, not the political optics, but her cosmetic dentistry. The contrast with Selina's military bravado ('I'm going in') works as a quick cringe beat.

jokes.text: Here he is, Kanye West Wing.
jokes.setup: Jonah arrives at the Congressional hearing area.
jokes.punchline: Jonah's self-introduction as 'Kanye West Wing' — a portmanteau of Kanye West and West Wing — is delivered with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: A tight pun that works on multiple levels: Kanye's narcissism mapped onto Jonah's, 'West Wing' as White House shorthand, all in three words. The character fit is excellent — only Jonah would introduce himself this way to a Congressional staffer.

jokes.text: No, I'm Cliff. I'm Sue's Sue. — I'm the boy named Sue.
jokes.setup: Jonah assumes Cliff is Congressman Ramsey. Cliff explains he's Sue's fill-in.
jokes.punchline: 'I'm the boy named Sue' — a Johnny Cash reference, delivered as a self-description for a man named Cliff filling in for a woman named Sue.
jokes.explanation: The 'boy named Sue' reference works contextually (Cliff is a man named Sue in the functional sense) and is delivered with Cliff's characteristic terrible joke instinct. His need to explain it by saying 'What?' (Jonah's response) is another beat that underlines Cliff's comedic incompetence.

jokes.text: Cliff, when I said no jokes, that wasn't a joke.
jokes.setup: Cliff makes the Johnny Cash joke despite Gary having told him not to joke.
jokes.punchline: Jonah echoes Gary's instruction with a meta statement: 'when I said no jokes, that wasn't a joke.'
jokes.explanation: A callback to Gary's earlier instruction that lands because Cliff ignored it immediately. The meta phrasing — 'when I said no jokes, that wasn't a joke' — has a pleasing logical loop. Rewatch bonus for catching the callback.

jokes.text: Keep it dull, which incidentally, is also dull.
jokes.setup: Jonah instructs Sue to keep her congressional testimony dull.
jokes.punchline: Sue dryly observes that the instruction itself is dull — a meta-commentary on the dullness they're attempting to perform.
jokes.explanation: Sue rarely gets jokes but this is a superb one. Her ability to find the logical flaw in any situation is compressed perfectly here: the meta-observation that a directive about dullness is itself dull, delivered in Sue's signature flat affect. Pure character.

jokes.text: The only people watching this are you and some guy in the hospital with locked-in syndrome.
jokes.setup: The congressional hearing begins. Dan wonders if Sue is nervous.
jokes.punchline: Jonah dismisses the audience for the hearing as consisting of him and a locked-in syndrome patient — the only person unable to change the channel.
jokes.explanation: Genuinely dark and genuinely funny. 'Locked-in syndrome' patient as the only captive audience for a boring congressional hearing is a horrific image that works because the logic is airtight: if you can't move or change the channel, you ARE the ideal viewer for C-SPAN. The specificity of the medical condition elevates it above a generic 'nobody's watching' joke.

jokes.text: My pulse is racing, sir.
jokes.setup: The hearing begins to ask about budget percentages. Sue is presumably performing boredom.
jokes.punchline: Sue deadpans 'My pulse is racing, sir' — the opposite of what she means, delivered with her characteristic absolute flatness.
jokes.explanation: Four words, maximum Sue. Her deadpan 'My pulse is racing, sir' in response to questions about budget percentages is perfect: it simultaneously signals total boredom and commits to the irony with zero wink. Sue's deadpan works because she never breaks.

jokes.text: If you don't mind my saying, ma'am, there speaketh a civilian. — All right. There speaketh a pricketh.
jokes.setup: Selina says 'This is fun, isn't it?' while being instructed on firearm safety. The Marine archly observes she sounds like a civilian.
jokes.punchline: Selina matches his faux-archaic register — 'there speaketh a pricketh' — inventing a fake Middle English suffix to call him a prick.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. The Marine uses 'speaketh' as condescension; Selina turns it back with perfect structural symmetry — same construction, but 'pricketh' inventing a fake Old English conjugation. The '-eth' suffix on 'prick' is a genuinely original comic construction, hilarious on paper and even better in delivery. This is what 8.5 looks like: specific, structurally tight, character-perfect, and quotable.

jokes.text: Oh, Jesus fucking Christ! (after firing and presumably missing badly or getting recoil)
jokes.setup: Selina takes her first shot at the range.
jokes.punchline: Her immediate profane outburst, followed by the revelation that she actually got a 'tremendous shot.'
jokes.explanation: Works primarily as a physical/reaction beat — she's surprised by the gun, swears loudly in front of Marines, and turns out to be a good shot anyway. The comedy is in the misdirection (we expect failure, we get success). Functional but not particularly crafted.

jokes.text: These fellas have heard a lot worse than that, ma'am. — Tremendous shot, by the way.
jokes.setup: Selina has just screamed 'Jesus fucking Christ' on a military base after firing.
jokes.punchline: The Marine's complete composure — reassuring her about the profanity then immediately complimenting her shooting — is funnier than any outburst.
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of total professionalism with Selina's chaos is a recurring beat in this sequence. Works as a reaction beat; nothing exceptional in the writing but effective in context.

jokes.text: Oh, how about that? Fastest gun in the West Wing.
jokes.setup: Marines compliment Selina on her shot.
jokes.punchline: 'Fastest gun in the West Wing' — a pun combining 'fastest gun in the West' with West Wing as White House shorthand.
jokes.explanation: Clean, functional pun. Works in the moment as a pleased Selina callback to the setting. The repetition of 'West Wing' as a pun vessel (cf. 'Kanye West Wing') shows the writers working that seam. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: With this hostage situation, I don't think you have the intelligence. — As in intel, of course, ma'am.
jokes.setup: Maddox tells Selina she doesn't have 'the intelligence' regarding the hostage situation.
jokes.punchline: He immediately clarifies 'as in intel' — but the clarification confirms she heard it correctly the first time (as an insult about her intellectual capacity) and he's just covering.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully constructed double meaning — 'intelligence' as intellect AND as security intel. Maddox gets to deliver a devastating insult AND immediately claim plausible deniability, which is exactly his character. The 'of course' in the clarification makes it worse — it insists she MUST have known he meant intel. Excellent structure, high quotability. Earns the 8.0.

jokes.text: Is it four or is it five? Or is it six? Or is it seven? You don't know, do you?
jokes.setup: Maddox questions Selina's knowledge of countries neighboring Uzbekistan.
jokes.punchline: Selina turns it back on him, rapidly escalating the possible answer to demonstrate that SHE knows and HE doesn't — then the reveal that she just guessed.
jokes.explanation: The escalation ('four... five... six... seven') is a fake-out — she's bluffing by suggesting there are many possibilities. The beat after ('It's five') paired with the immediate reveal that she 'pulled it straight out of her ass' creates a perfect two-step.

jokes.text: I pulled that straight out of my ass. — You were born for that moment. — I know. He's Iwo Jima. I'm planting a flag right on his head.
jokes.setup: Selina has successfully bluffed Maddox on the Uzbekistan question.
jokes.punchline: She immediately admits it was improvised BS, then follows with a triumphant Iwo Jima metaphor about planting a flag on Maddox's head.
jokes.explanation: The Iwo Jima image is both absurd and perfectly apt — Selina's political victories are as hard-won and militarily framed as a famous Pacific battle. Delivered with enormous self-satisfaction, which is core Selina.

jokes.text: Oh, shit a shark.
jokes.setup: The congressional hearing has pivoted to asking about the waste of two motorcades — exactly what they were trying to avoid.
jokes.punchline: 'Shit a shark' as a profane expression of dread — a distinctive Veep-ism that's specific and funny in its unexpectedness.
jokes.explanation: 'Shit a shark' is a memorable profane coinage — it's in the Veep tradition of inventive scatological expressions. The unexpectedness of 'shark' (rather than a more conventional object) is what makes it work. Very quotable for a reaction line.

jokes.text: Sir, we have a system that I believe to be robust. — Robust? — Robust. — Our management team is robust.
jokes.setup: Under pressure at the hearing, Sue defaults to the only power word she's been primed with.
jokes.punchline: She uses 'robust' twice in quick succession, doubling down as it becomes more conspicuous.
jokes.explanation: The 'robust' running gag pays off here in a cringe crescendo. Sue — told to be dull — latches onto the one word she's heard all morning and deploys it in the worst possible context. The escalation across two uses ('robust… robust') while Hessler grows increasingly suspicious is expertly constructed. One of the better running gag payoffs in the episode.

jokes.text: Okay, now she just sounds mentally ill.
jokes.setup: Sue has used 'robust' three times in about thirty seconds at the hearing.
jokes.punchline: Dan's clinical outside-observer reaction — she has moved past bad strategy into apparent psychiatric symptom.
jokes.explanation: Dan's commentary functions as the audience's proxy reaction — the joke is that he articulates exactly what we're thinking. 'Mentally ill' is funnier than 'crazy' because it's more clinical. Short, well-timed, high impact.

jokes.text: I'm working remotely.
jokes.setup: Cliff challenges why Jonah is at the hearing location instead of the White House.
jokes.punchline: Jonah says 'I'm working remotely' — as if standing in the congressional building counts as remote work.
jokes.explanation: A quick, efficient Jonah moment — his entitled obliviousness compressed into three words. The joke depends on knowing that 'remote' means different things in 2013 vs. post-pandemic, but in context it's clearly self-serving nonsense.

jokes.text: I'm guessing you have one of those word-a-day calendars. And I know what the word for today is. It's robust.
jokes.setup: Sue has used 'robust' repeatedly. Hessler turns the interrogation toward the word choice.
jokes.punchline: He calls her out on the word-a-day framing — a cutting way of saying she's mechanically deploying a coached term.
jokes.explanation: Hessler weaponizing the running gag against Sue — the 'word-a-day calendar' construction is sharp because it implies deliberate inanity. The punchline ('I know what the word for today is') has the rhythm of a late-night joke.

jokes.text: Well, I'm guessing that you think you have a sense of humor, Mr. Hessler. Which you are wrong about, as you are about the calendar. That is my definition of robust.
jokes.setup: Hessler mocks Sue with the word-a-day calendar joke.
jokes.punchline: Sue dismantles him with complete composure: denies him both the sense-of-humor attribution AND the calendar premise, then redefines 'robust' as this takedown itself.
jokes.explanation: Sue's best moment in the series — and one of the episode's peaks. She simultaneously: (1) denies Hessler a sense of humor, (2) denies the calendar premise, and (3) reframes her own word choice as an example of robustness. It's a meta-comeback that ends the running gag by claiming it. 'That is my definition of robust' is a perfect mic-drop that's also exactly what a Sue who has no sense of humor would say. Near-perfect character integration — no other character could deliver this. Earns 8.4 craft.

jokes.text: My speech is a face-melter. These buzz cuts are gonna flip.
jokes.setup: Selina is preparing to give her speech to the Marines.
jokes.punchline: 'Face-melter' as a descriptor for a political speech, and 'buzz cuts are gonna flip' as a way of saying Marines will be excited.
jokes.explanation: Fun Selina self-aggrandizement. 'Buzz cuts are gonna flip' is a slightly awkward construction that's funnier for being slightly awkward. Not a standout but characterful.

jokes.text: It's time to robust a nut all over this place.
jokes.setup: The team is panicking about the robust situation.
jokes.punchline: Dan coins 'robust a nut' as a verb phrase, reclaiming 'robust' as action while vulgarizing it.
jokes.explanation: Using 'robust' as a verb to describe a sexual act is a creative escalation of the running gag — it's now been so overloaded that it can be repurposed entirely. Dan's enthusiasm makes it land.

jokes.text: Explosive. Punchy. Fearless. I'm just saying words.
jokes.setup: The team is desperately searching for a synonym for 'robust' that Selina can use in her speech.
jokes.punchline: Mike's self-aware admission that he's just listing words without any editorial judgment.
jokes.explanation: Pure Mike. The self-aware 'I'm just saying words' is an accidental confession of incompetence delivered as if it's a reasonable contribution to the brainstorm. It retroactively makes all the words he said equally meaningless.

jokes.text: Gary, I need the speech. I need the Vice President's ear. — Get your own ear, van Gogh. — Back off.
jokes.setup: Dan tries to intercept Selina to change the speech before she delivers it.
jokes.punchline: Gary tells Dan to 'get your own ear, van Gogh' — a reference to van Gogh's severed ear suggesting Dan should cut off his own rather than take Gary's access to Selina.
jokes.explanation: A creative, specific insult — Gary's protectiveness of his 'VP ear access' expressed via art history is unexpected. 'Van Gogh' does double duty: it references ear removal AND implies Dan should self-mutilate, which is appropriately extreme for Gary's possessiveness.

jokes.text: There goes the R-bomb. — I thought we weren't doing robust. — I thought we were still on considered.
jokes.setup: Selina has just used 'robust' in her speech after the team switched to 'strong.'
jokes.punchline: The team's simultaneous, overlapping realizations that she used the wrong word — 'the R-bomb' as a coinage.
jokes.explanation: The 'R-bomb' coinage is the episode's funniest compression of the running gag — treating a political adjective like a nuclear weapon. The overlapping different versions ('robust' vs 'considered' vs 'strong') reveal how chaotic the coordination has been.

jokes.text: Stick a thermometer up Washington's ass. See how much heat I just created. — Parting the cheeks.
jokes.setup: Selina is pleased with her speech and asks Mike to gauge the reaction.
jokes.punchline: She asks for temperature via rectal thermometer metaphor; Mike's 'Parting the cheeks' confirms he'll perform the metaphorical task with complete seriousness.
jokes.explanation: Veep's signature bodily-function political metaphor — Mike's total commitment to playing along with 'Parting the cheeks' is the key addition. It extends the image enough to get a second laugh.

jokes.text: MIKE: You don't look constipated. You look the opposite of it. — SELINA: What's the opposite of constipated, Mike? — Relieved. — No. Relieved is not the opposite of constipated, Mike. Hang up the phone, dummy.
jokes.setup: Selina is upset about a photo making her look constipated. Mike tries to reassure her by naming the opposite condition.
jokes.punchline: Mike says 'relieved' — which is actually the correct antonym for constipated (relief = having a bowel movement), but Selina rejects it because she's heard it only as an emotional term. The joke is that Mike is technically correct and she's wrong.
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully constructed joke. The setup is Selina's petty vanity; the joke hinges on 'relieved' being BOTH a valid antonym for constipated AND an emotional state — Mike is correct physiologically, but Selina assumes he's making an emotional statement ('you look relieved'). The depth of the misunderstanding and the 'Hang up the phone, dummy' capper make this one of the episode's best exchanges. Earns 8+ for structural precision and rewatch value.

jokes.text: VP sister act robusting the moves
jokes.setup: The team reads a Post headline about Selina's speech.
jokes.punchline: The headline uses 'robusting' as a verb — the word has now spread and mutated in the press.
jokes.explanation: The word having escaped into the media ecosystem and mutated into a verb ('robusting') is a good payoff for the running gag. The headline itself is funny in the tabloid-ese style.

jokes.text: Word cloud scene: 'robust' dominates, 'Sue' visible, Selina's name is tiny — 'It's bigger than collapse.'
jokes.setup: The team reviews a word cloud from DC press. Selina looks for her own name.
jokes.punchline: Her name 'Selina' is tiny; when they finally find it, Amy's consolation is 'It's bigger than collapse.'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most layered visual/character jokes. The word cloud is a brilliant political satire device — Selina's narcissism collides with the humiliating reality that no one is talking about her specifically. 'It's bigger than collapse' is the perfect consolation that isn't a consolation: the word 'collapse' being in the national political vocabulary at all is an indictment, and hers is bigger. Earns 8.0.

jokes.text: I mean, Sue only has one name like Pocahontas or Hitler. — Or Moby or Bigfoot.
jokes.setup: Amy explains why 'Selina' is small in the word cloud — she has four names (Vice President Selina Meyer).
jokes.punchline: Amy uses Hitler as a famous one-name figure alongside Pocahontas; Selina adds Moby and Bigfoot.
jokes.explanation: The Hitler name-drop lands because it's smuggled in alongside genuinely innocent one-name figures (Pocahontas, Moby, Bigfoot). The escalation into Bigfoot as a comparator is absurdist in the best way. Two laughs from one list.

jokes.text: What? You're not fucking Sue. — I'm Cliff. I'm filling in for Sue.
jokes.setup: Selina, distracted by the word cloud, calls for Sue.
jokes.punchline: The reminder that it's Cliff — which the show has now set up as a recurring comedic reveal.
jokes.explanation: A functional running gag beat — the comedy of Selina repeatedly forgetting Cliff is there. Not crafted so much as reliable; the running gag has diminishing returns by this point.

jokes.text: She has a return appearance? What does she need an encore for? — Yeah, it's an encore. Start popping your popcorn.
jokes.setup: Cliff mentions Sue has a 'return appearance' at the hearing.
jokes.punchline: Selina's contemptuous framing of Sue's legal obligation as if it's a victory lap, then Amy's 'Start popping your popcorn' — treating it as must-see TV.
jokes.explanation: A compact exchange that shows the team's competitive paranoia about each other — Sue's mandatory hearing becomes Selina's competitive threat. 'Start popping your popcorn' as political sarcasm works but doesn't land as a memorable line.

jokes.text: Gary-oke time. — It is. — Danny-oke? — End of an era, buddy. End of an era.
jokes.setup: Gary has prepared his senator swearing-in cards. Dan pitches an alternative approach.
jokes.punchline: Selina agrees with Dan over Gary — and Dan gloats with 'End of an era, buddy. End of an era' while Gary is clearly devastated.
jokes.explanation: Gary-oke as a portmanteau is mildly clever; the cringe comedy comes from Gary's genuine hurt at being displaced. Dan's repetition of 'End of an era' (twice) is exactly the kind of cruelty that defines his character. The pathos under the joke gives it texture.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, me and Kim Kardashian. Ha ha.
jokes.setup: Someone says they're not famous (or only famous in DC).
jokes.punchline: Dan name-drops Kim Kardashian as a comparable famous person — with complete irony since DC insider fame is nothing like celebrity fame.
jokes.explanation: A weak joke — celebrity name-drop as punchline is exactly the kind of shortcut the scoring guidelines flag. The comparison works on a basic level but brings nothing specific or fresh.

jokes.text: Does aggressive trump robust? — Well, it's robustier. — No, robust is like a rock and aggressive is like paper. — Oh, shoot, paper covers rock. I forget about that.
jokes.setup: Maddox has used 'aggressive' in a media interview. The team debates whether this word is politically more powerful than 'robust.'
jokes.punchline: Ben analogizes the conflict as rock-paper-scissors — then Selina realizes paper beats rock and is genuinely dismayed.
jokes.explanation: A superb multi-part joke. First: 'robustier' — Dan coining a comparative of a word that shouldn't have one. Second: Ben applying rock-paper-scissors to political adjectives. Third: Selina's genuine upset at realizing paper covers rock — she is applying the game's logic to her political situation with total sincerity. The escalating absurdism earns it.

jokes.text: His word is 'get-the-vice-president-over-here-right-now-we're-having-a-fucking-meeting.' It's all one word hyphenated.
jokes.setup: The team asks what Ben's 'word' is for the response — they've been playing the synonym game.
jokes.punchline: Amy reads Ben's text as if it's a single hyphenated word entry in the synonym competition.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. Amy plays it completely straight — reading Ben's enraged text as if it's a legitimate synonym submission, with 'It's all one word hyphenated' as the capper. The structural conceit (the synonym competition) sets it up perfectly, and the delivery format (treating rage as vocabulary) is genuinely original. Very high quotability. Earns 8+

jokes.text: They're different words. And saying different words means what? It means we're not on the same... — Diet? — Page.
jokes.setup: Ben is trying to explain that using different words is a political problem.
jokes.punchline: Selina completes his sentence with 'Diet' — a non sequitur that implies she was only half-listening (or thinking about food).
jokes.explanation: A clean misdirection — the setup clearly leads to 'page,' and 'diet' is left-field enough to be funny while also being revealing about Selina's attention span. Ben's exasperated 'Page' capper doesn't overexplain.

jokes.text: Or to Lara Croft here for that matter.
jokes.setup: Ben asserts authority; Selina says she doesn't have to answer to him or to Kent.
jokes.punchline: Ben calls Kent 'Lara Croft' — presumably because of Kent's stiff, action-figure demeanor or the way he presents himself.
jokes.explanation: A decent quick insult — 'Lara Croft' works because it implies Kent is an unrealistic, overdetermined action figure rather than a human being. Better than a generic insult but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Oh, where is the great and powerful Oz, by the way?
jokes.setup: Ben reminds Selina that they all work for the President.
jokes.punchline: Selina sarcastically refers to POTUS as 'the great and powerful Oz' — invisible, manipulative, and possibly illusory.
jokes.explanation: A functional pop culture reference that maps reasonably well (the hidden President as the man behind the curtain). Not original — the Oz comparison for invisible authority figures is common — but delivered well.

jokes.text: Calculated? That sounds so... — Pussy-ass, that's what I was gonna say.
jokes.setup: Ben proposes 'calculated response' as the new agreed term. Selina starts to object.
jokes.punchline: Selina presumably was searching for a diplomatic objection; Ben just says 'pussy-ass' as her completion — which is absolutely what she meant.
jokes.explanation: Ben finishing Selina's polite objection with her actual crude thought is a neat bit of character mirroring — they're more aligned than they like to admit. The misdirection (waiting for Selina's diplomatic phrasing, getting Ben's blunt version) works well.

jokes.text: She fires a gun one time and she thinks she's Rommel in the desert.
jokes.setup: Selina advocates for more aggressive military action, excited by her shooting performance.
jokes.punchline: Ben compares her sudden military hawkishness — after one range exercise — to Rommel, Nazi Germany's most celebrated general.
jokes.explanation: Rommel is a specific and funny choice — not just a generic military figure but the 'Desert Fox' specifically, which maps onto the Marine base/shooting context. Ben's eye-rolling deployment of historical military comparison is perfectly his voice.

jokes.text: Yeah, I was talking to POTUS last night... He's gonna procrasterbate forever.
jokes.setup: The team worries POTUS will change his mind if given too much time.
jokes.punchline: 'Procrasterbate' — procrastinate + masturbate — as a coinage for the President's indecisive self-pleasuring inaction.
jokes.explanation: A genuine neologism that earns its place. 'Procrasterbate' fuses procrastination with masturbation to create a word that means 'self-indulgent indecision' — and it's perfectly applicable to a vacillating president. The portmanteau is clean (the syllables blend naturally), it's highly quotable beyond the show, and it's original. Earns 8.5.

jokes.text: Cock-a-noodle-doo.
jokes.setup: Kent announces that noodle-timing analysis means they can move on the hostages.
jokes.punchline: 'Cock-a-noodle-doo' — a portmanteau of 'cockadoodledoo' and 'noodle' — celebrates Kent's noodle-based strategic victory.
jokes.explanation: The noodle analogy callback arrives in the most economical possible form — a three-syllable invented word that references both the noodle analogy and a celebratory animal call. 'Cock-a-noodle-doo' is the kind of spontaneous wordplay that feels genuinely inspired. Ben's uncharacteristic exuberance (he's normally only exhausted) makes it funnier. High quotability.

jokes.text: We can move on the hostages. — Cock-a-noodle-doo.
jokes.setup: Kent announces the polling has reached the threshold — they can move on the hostages.
jokes.punchline: Ben's exclamation of 'Cock-a-noodle-doo' — a portmanteau of 'cock-a-doodle-doo' (triumph/dawn) and 'noodle' (Kent's earlier analogy).
jokes.explanation: A brilliantly economical callback — Ben's cynicism has actually absorbed Kent's insufferable noodle analogy and converted it into a triumphant crow. The portmanteau is clean and funny. It also represents Ben's rare moment of genuine excitement (the noodles are done!), which adds character. High quotability and earns rewatch bonus.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. I'd kiss you right now, but I'm never gonna do that.
jokes.setup: Kent announces they can proceed with the hostage operation.
jokes.punchline: Selina's impulse to celebrate is immediately checked by her complete revulsion at the thought of kissing Kent.
jokes.explanation: The immediate and unambiguous 'but I'm never gonna do that' is the joke — not just that she wouldn't, but the absolute certainty in the phrasing. Clean character moment.

jokes.text: Office of the Vice President. — This is the Vice President. — Yes, it is. — No, no, this is the Vice President.
jokes.setup: Selina calls her own office.
jokes.punchline: The operator says 'Yes, it is' in automatic agreement before Selina has to insist again — suggesting Cliff has already trained himself to agree with everything from the Vice President's line.
jokes.explanation: A good beat — the comic rhythm of 'Yes, it is / No, no, this is...' is a tiny Abbott and Costello loop. Selina having to argue that she is herself is a nice piece of institutional absurdity.

jokes.text: Set your DVR. I'm thinking of... Okay, you hung up.
jokes.setup: Cliff tells Selina about Sue's return hearing date while she's on the phone. She hangs up abruptly.
jokes.punchline: Cliff was mid-sentence pitching the hearing as must-see TV when the line goes dead.
jokes.explanation: A neat little beat — Cliff's earnest TV pitch collapsed by the hang-up. 'Okay, you hung up' delivered to a dead line reveals his Cliff-ness: he completes the realization aloud to no one.

jokes.text: Amy, this is like explaining gravity to a chicken.
jokes.setup: Cliff fails to understand why the senator swearing-in is immovable. Selina explains it to Amy.
jokes.punchline: 'Explaining gravity to a chicken' — a vivid, original simile for futile explanation.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best single-line jokes. 'Explaining gravity to a chicken' is a genuinely original simile — it evokes both the futility (a chicken cannot understand gravity) and the specific kind of stupid (chickens can't fly far despite having wings, suggesting confused relationship with the concept). It's vivid, compressed, and Selina-specific in its contemptuous precision. Earns 8.5.

jokes.text: You have made a fuck-up the size of France and you are fucking grinning about it. — I'm trying not to. I'm not sure why my face is doing this.
jokes.setup: Cliff has failed to flag the senator ceremony conflict, and is apparently smiling despite Selina's fury.
jokes.punchline: Cliff's complete inability to control his own facial expression — 'I'm not sure why my face is doing this' — while being screamed at.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful cringe comedy beat. Cliff's involuntary smiling during Selina's rage is already funny; his meta-commentary on it ('I'm not sure why my face is doing this') is perfect — it's simultaneously self-aware and helpless. 'Fuck-up the size of France' is also a memorable insult, specific enough to distinguish from generic large-scale comparisons.

jokes.text: This is all Cliff's fault, that grinning fucking Ewok.
jokes.setup: Selina is stuck with the senator ceremony while the situation room action happens without her.
jokes.punchline: 'Grinning fucking Ewok' — Selina weaponizes Cliff's involuntary smile and his presumably short/adorable appearance into a precise insult.
jokes.explanation: 'Grinning fucking Ewok' is a superb insult. The 'grinning' callback is precise (referencing his face issue), and 'Ewok' does triple duty: they're small, cute, and forest-dwelling (not in the action), which maps perfectly onto Cliff being a harmless, goofy outsider who doesn't belong in the real game. High quotability. Earns 8.0.

jokes.text: I feel like that porn star who had to do 200 men in one day. At least she got to lie down, right?
jokes.setup: Selina faces swearing in 33 senators while the hostage mission unfolds.
jokes.punchline: She compares herself to a prolific porn performer, then notes the (small) advantage the porn star had over her.
jokes.explanation: A signature Selina transgression — a sitting VP casually invoking pornography in a professional setting. The 'at least she got to lie down' capper is the genuine joke: she's jealous of the porn star's working conditions. Dark, character-specific, and lands well.

jokes.text: This isn't a POTUS thing. Why is scrotus here? — Think of me as a cheerleader, Mike. — Oh, God. I'm imagining you in a bathtub full of rose petals.
jokes.setup: Jonah shows up at the swearing-in ceremony. Mike is confused why he's there.
jokes.punchline: 'Scrotus' as a portmanteau for 'Senior Correspondent to the United States' (i.e., Jonah's title) — then Mike's 'bathtub full of rose petals' as an involuntary, horrifying mental image.
jokes.explanation: A two-for-one joke. 'Scrotus' is a running Veep joke — Jonah's acronym title sounds like 'scrotum' + POTUS — but what elevates it here is Mike's immediate, horrified 'bathtub full of rose petals.' Mike has conjured a Jonah cheerleader in a romantic bath setting and is visibly suffering. The combination of the portmanteau and Mike's involuntary imagery is very funny.

jokes.text: I want to know how all your filmmaking is going. — Beg pardon? — And so on. — Your fishing.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to connect with a senator using Gary's briefing cards, but misreads them.
jokes.punchline: She says 'filmmaking' when the card probably says 'fishing' — then covers with 'And so on' and 'Your fishing' as if nothing happened.
jokes.explanation: A good cringe beat — Selina's improvised recovery ('And so on' as a bridge from filmmaking to fishing) is painfully smooth in a way that makes it worse. The senator's bewildered 'Beg pardon?' lands the embarrassment.

jokes.text: Oh, well, that's all we want. We all want the big one, don't we? — Did she just make an innuendo? — Yep. Yep.
jokes.setup: A senator says he's 'still hunting for that big one' about fishing.
jokes.punchline: Selina responds with 'We all want the big one, don't we?' — an accidental innuendo she apparently doesn't notice.
jokes.explanation: A reliable accidental innuendo beat — the comedy is in Amy and Dan's weary 'Yep. Yep.' reaction, which implies this happens often. Not particularly original but executed cleanly. Works better as a character beat than a written joke.

jokes.text: That I will bear true faith and allegiance to the same... so help me Brian.
jokes.setup: Selina is swearing in Senator Brian — she's clearly distracted by the situation room.
jokes.punchline: She concludes the Constitutional oath with 'so help me Brian' instead of 'so help me God.'
jokes.explanation: A solid cringe moment — Selina substitutes 'God' with the senator's first name during a sacred oath of office. The follow-up exchange ('I am Brian. I know. I think you mean so help you God. Yes yes. So help me God. Me, God, yes.') escalates the awkwardness perfectly.

jokes.text: I think you mean 'so help you God.' — Yes, yes. Oh, so help you God. — So help me God. — Me, God, yes.
jokes.setup: Selina has said 'so help me Brian.' The senator helpfully corrects her.
jokes.punchline: The correction spirals — 'so help you God' vs 'so help me God' — and Selina's 'Me, God, yes' is a complete collapse of the oath.
jokes.explanation: The escalation of the oath error is well-constructed — what starts as a simple mistake becomes a grammatical constitutional crisis. 'Me, God, yes' as a final utterance has the rhythm of someone who has fully lost the thread. The escalation is the joke.

jokes.text: Oh, I wish that was my cousin's name. — This is the worst small talk I've ever heard. And I'm including mine in that and mine is horrible. She's done worse. I can't think of when, but she has.
jokes.setup: A child named Ruby is brought in for a photo. Selina's response is 'Oh, I wish that was my cousin's name.'
jokes.punchline: Dan and Amy's running commentary — Dan acknowledges his own small talk is terrible, still worse than Selina's; Amy insists she's done worse but can't name a time.
jokes.explanation: Dan's 'I'm including mine in that and mine is horrible' is self-aware and funny — he's damning himself to save Selina, then the 'She's done worse / I can't think of when' loop is a perfectly constructed two-step that keeps the cringe going. Good running observer commentary.

jokes.text: Well, that's how the cookie crumbles.
jokes.setup: A senator mentions they went skiing in Vermont for New Year's.
jokes.punchline: Selina responds with 'Well, that's how the cookie crumbles' — a non sequitur expression that makes zero sense as a response to skiing.
jokes.explanation: Selina is so distracted that she's deploying meaningless filler phrases. 'That's how the cookie crumbles' as a response to a pleasant holiday anecdote is a sign of total mental absence. Works as part of the sustained cringe of the swearing-in sequence.

jokes.text: Amy, it's time o'clock. — What's time o'clock? — Time o'clock.
jokes.setup: Selina is trying to signal to Amy that it's time to move to the secure video teleconference.
jokes.punchline: 'Time o'clock' as a made-up time expression — Selina is inventing code language in real time because she can't say anything classified.
jokes.explanation: 'Time o'clock' is a lovely piece of improvised absurdism — it sounds like a real phrase for a fraction of a second, then collapses into meaninglessness. Amy's genuine 'What's time o'clock?' confirms that even her closest aide has no idea what she means. High quotability for its nonsense economy.

jokes.text: Maybe she's pregnant again. — Oh, God. — Or she's menopausal. — Oh, God, she's gonna want that testosterone gel. I'm out of here.
jokes.setup: The team speculates about why Selina is behaving strangely during the swearing-in ceremony.
jokes.punchline: Gary escalates from pregnancy to menopause to testosterone gel — and Gary's 'I'm out of here' at the thought of managing that is the kicker.
jokes.explanation: A quick escalation joke — each possibility gets worse for the people who have to manage it. Gary fleeing at 'testosterone gel' is the payoff and has good character specificity.

jokes.text: Wait a minute. Jonah's going in? — The men get the situation room, I get the shituation room?
jokes.setup: Selina watches Jonah get access to the actual situation room while she's stuck with the senators.
jokes.punchline: 'Shituation room' — a portmanteau of 'shit' and 'situation room' — Selina's assessment of her virtual setup.
jokes.explanation: 'Shituation room' is a perfectly constructed portmanteau — clean, immediate, and devastatingly self-pitying. The men/shituation room dichotomy also has a political dimension (gender dynamics in power) that adds a second layer. High quotability. One of the episode's most memorable coinages.

jokes.text: Hi, gentlemen. — I hope you heard that joke. That was meant for you to hear it.
jokes.setup: Selina connects to the virtual sit room and makes a quip ('shituation room') for the benefit of the generals.
jokes.punchline: She immediately clarifies that the joke was intentional and meant to be heard — completely undermining any cool factor the quip might have had.
jokes.explanation: The meta-joke here is Selina's compulsive need for approval: she makes a quip, then immediately explains it was a quip. This is a perfect character moment — Selina can't resist performing even when she's trying to appear presidential. 'That was meant for you to hear it' is exactly what you say when a joke doesn't land and you can't let it go.

jokes.text: This is so awesome. I'll bet you camera three gets a kill.
jokes.setup: The military operation begins, with Jonah watching live feeds from the Black Hawks.
jokes.punchline: Jonah treats the live footage of a military operation as if it's a sporting event — betting on which camera will capture a kill.
jokes.explanation: Dark and character-perfect for Jonah — he's so disconnected from the gravity of live military action that he responds to it like a video game spectator. 'Camera three gets a kill' is specific enough to be genuinely unsettling and funny.

jokes.text: When the guns go off, they will be loud, Madam Vice President, so no swearing.
jokes.setup: The military operation is about to commence over the secure video link.
jokes.punchline: Maddox's condescending warning — referencing Selina's earlier 'Jesus fucking Christ' at the range — deployed in the most high-stakes possible moment.
jokes.explanation: Excellent callback — Maddox weaponizes Selina's shooting range profanity in the actual operation, doubling as political point-scoring and genuine condescension. 'So no swearing' is delivered with perfect timing right before the operation begins.

jokes.text: George, comments like that are incredibly inappropriate when people's lives are at stake. — I've got to get a refill here. Old jumbo needs a refill.
jokes.setup: Selina delivers a principled rebuke to Maddox during the live operation.
jokes.punchline: Jonah, mid-operation, interrupts to announce he needs a drink refill — calling his glass 'Old Jumbo.'
jokes.explanation: Jonah treating a live military operation with the attention span of someone at a sporting event, announcing a drink refill for 'Old Jumbo,' is a perfectly absurd character beat. The naming of his glass 'Old Jumbo' is the specific touch that makes it funnier than a generic interruption.

jokes.text: The eggs are back in the basket! — Yeah! Eggs-shak-a-laka! — In your fucking face, eggs.
jokes.setup: The hostage rescue succeeds. A soldier announces it in military code.
jokes.punchline: The euphemistic 'eggs back in the basket' triggers Selina's 'Eggs-shak-a-laka!' — then Ben's 'In your fucking face, eggs' addresses the metaphorical eggs directly.
jokes.explanation: 'Eggs-shak-a-laka' as a celebratory neologism from Selina is good, but 'In your fucking face, eggs' is the line — Ben directly confronting the metaphorical eggs as his enemy is an absurdist breakdown of the euphemism that works brilliantly. High quotability.

jokes.text: Legacy marker. Yeah!
jokes.setup: The hostage rescue is confirmed successful. Everyone celebrates.
jokes.punchline: Dan, in the middle of a life-or-death operation's success, immediately frames it as a 'legacy marker' — political capital first, human lives second.
jokes.explanation: Dan's 'Legacy marker' is pure character — his brain instantly converts human triumph into political calculus. The enthusiasm of 'Yeah!' is the cruelest part: he's genuinely excited, just not about what everyone else is excited about.

jokes.text: So Sue's return to the hearing's wiped off the news map. That's the last thing on anyone's mind today.
jokes.setup: The hostage rescue has succeeded. Amy notes the political side-effect.
jokes.punchline: The episode's central political intrigue — that Selina chose the operation date to overshadow Sue — is confirmed in Amy's casually delivered observation.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the episode's darkest comic premise — a VP used a military operation to bury a rival's news cycle. Amy's matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier and more damning than any outrage would.

jokes.text: Ashes to ashes, robust to dust.
jokes.setup: Hessler watches as Sue's hearing is drowned out by the hostage rescue news.
jokes.punchline: 'Ashes to ashes, robust to dust' — a funeral parody with 'robust' replacing 'dust,' marking the word's death as a political signal.
jokes.explanation: The episode's single best joke and the perfect cap to the 'robust' running gag. The religious funeral formula ('ashes to ashes') is subverted by substituting 'robust' for 'dust' — delivering both a pun (robust/dust as political currency) and a genuine ending to the word's arc. The parallelism is metrically perfect: 'ashes to ashes / robust to dust' scans exactly like the original. It works as a joke, a callback, a thematic statement, and a brilliant piece of writing. High craft across every dimension. Earns 8.7.

jokes.text: Pumped and dumped by the fame monster, huh?
jokes.setup: Mike greets Sue returning from her cut-short hearing.
jokes.punchline: 'Pumped and dumped by the fame monster' — a sexual metaphor for brief media celebrity that Mike deploys with complete cheerfulness.
jokes.explanation: A crisp coinage — 'pumped and dumped by the fame monster' is an inventive way to describe viral fame's short shelf life. Mike's cheerful delivery is dark in context since Sue was exploited by the same political machinery he works for.

jokes.text: Yeah! Semper fucking fee. — Fi. — Fi. Fi, right.
jokes.setup: Operation confirmed total success. Selina celebrates with the Marine Corps motto.
jokes.punchline: She gets 'Semper Fi' wrong — 'Semper fee' — then has to be corrected twice before accepting 'Fi' with the energy of someone who still isn't sure.
jokes.explanation: A nice callback to Selina's persistent failure to actually know military culture despite her performative enthusiasm for it all episode. 'Semper fucking fee' is the signature combination of Veep profanity and incompetence.

jokes.text: You mean one of our Marines lost a leg? — Yes, ma'am. His left leg, ma'am. — The entire leg? — I believe he lost from above the knee. — Oh, so they were able to just salvage his foot?
jokes.setup: Selina is informed a Marine sergeant lost a leg in the operation.
jokes.punchline: 'Oh, so they were able to just salvage his foot?' — Selina's attempt to find a silver lining is a complete anatomical misunderstanding.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's two best jokes, and the darkest. Selina's complete failure to understand 'above the knee' — concluding that if the leg is gone above the knee, the foot must have been separately preserved — is both a devastating portrait of her self-protective optimism and genuinely funny in its anatomical absurdity. 'Salvage his foot' as a medical frame is horrifying and perfect. The slow escalation (left leg → entire leg? → above the knee → foot salvage?) is structurally impeccable. Earns 8.7.

jokes.text: Was he part of the operation? — Yes. He was actually due to be rotated out on the eighth.
jokes.setup: Selina asks if the injured Marine was part of the operation.
jokes.punchline: The NSC official notes he was due to rotate out on the eighth — meaning if Selina had chosen the ninth (the other option), he wouldn't have been there.
jokes.explanation: The episode's darkest comedic payoff — and it's not purely a joke, it's a structural indictment. The NSC's casual mention that the Marine 'was due to rotate out on the eighth' directly links Selina's politically-motivated date choice to his injury. This is Veep at its best: the comedy has real consequence, and the audience processes both the laugh and the horror simultaneously. The rewatch bonus is high — on second viewing, you understand the full weight immediately.

jokes.text: Ma'am, I just want to say consummate performance. Not only did you rescue the hostages, but you annihilated Sue. You picked the operation date to blow her appearance off the map. I mean, genius play, ma'am. Genius. I call that overshadowboxing.
jokes.setup: Dan congratulates Selina in the aftermath of the operation, immediately politicizing the human achievement.
jokes.punchline: 'Overshadowboxing' — a portmanteau of 'overshadowing' and 'shadowboxing' — as a political maneuver term for using one story to bury another.
jokes.explanation: 'Overshadowboxing' is a good coinage — it works as a political metaphor and has the phonetic satisfaction of the original word. Dan's complete obliviousness to the human cost (a leg) while congratulating Selina's genius play is darkly funny and deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: The problem with being so close to somebody's ear all the time is that you begin to think that you can see inside their head, but you can't, Dan. You can't.
jokes.setup: Dan has correctly deduced that Selina chose the sixth to bury Sue. She denies it.
jokes.punchline: Selina's elaborate denial is itself a confirmation — the more vehemently she insists Dan can't see inside her head, the clearer it is that he can.
jokes.explanation: A beautifully ironic moment — Selina's denial is structured as philosophical wisdom ('you can't see inside someone's head') but is transparently about her own guilt. The comedy is in the gap between her claimed insight and her obvious transparency. Character integration is high.

jokes.text: I lost a man his leg. — Please stop saying that, ma'am. — He'll bounce back. I got a feeling.
jokes.setup: Selina is racked with guilt over the Marine's leg. Gary tries to comfort her.
jokes.punchline: 'He'll bounce back. I got a feeling.' — Gary's cheerful, baseless optimism about a man who lost a leg above the knee.
jokes.explanation: Gary's cheerful inanity — 'I got a feeling' about a man's medical recovery — is both funny and a clear sign of Gary desperately managing Selina rather than engaging with reality. The dark irony of 'bounce back' for an amputee gives it a second layer.

jokes.text: The leg is just the price of admission to the place you wanted to be.
jokes.setup: Amy tries to contextualize the Marine's sacrifice for Selina.
jokes.punchline: Amy frames a man losing his leg as 'the price of admission' — a transactional, almost theatrical metaphor that's horrifying in its blitheness.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's darkest and most quotable lines. Amy's reframing of catastrophic human cost as the political equivalent of a ticket stub is the fullest expression of Veep's cynical worldview. Delivered with practical efficiency rather than cruelty — which makes it much worse (and funnier). Earns 8.0.

jokes.text: Well, maybe we could invite him to the White House. — Oh. — You know, once he's back up on his... — Sure, we'll roll out the ramps.
jokes.setup: Selina tries to make it up to the injured Marine by inviting him to the White House.
jokes.punchline: Her sentence trails off as she realizes she was about to say 'back up on his feet' — and Amy's 'roll out the ramps' immediately picks up the thread.
jokes.explanation: A beautiful structural joke: Selina's incomplete sentence ('back up on his...') is completed by Amy's wheelchair ramp reference. The timing of the interruption — at exactly the right moment — and Amy's complete absence of tact ('roll out the ramps') makes this an escalating dark comedy beat. Selina's mortified pause before Amy speaks is the setup. Earns 8.0+.

jokes.text: Ma'am, some of the prosthetic legs they have these days are as good as real legs. I guarantee you they're better than my shitty legs. I hate my legs. In fact, if I had the choice...
jokes.setup: Gary tries to comfort Selina about the Marine's amputation.
jokes.punchline: Gary's comforting fact about prosthetics spirals into a personal complaint about his own legs, heading toward the implication that he'd choose amputation — then catches himself.
jokes.explanation: Gary's absolute best moment in the episode. He starts with genuine comfort, slides into self-pity ('my shitty legs'), and then nearly says he'd voluntarily amputate — before catching himself. The interruption/self-catch is the funniest part: Gary realizes mid-sentence that he's just implied a wounded Marine has better body parts than him. Deep character work: Gary's devotion to Selina means his comfort attempts always orbit back to himself. Earns 8.4.

jokes.text: Can you make it a big one so you can stuff me inside of it and smuggle me out of here? — Of course I can. — And there might be a little raspberry surprise in them.
jokes.setup: The episode ends with Selina asking Gary for an almond croissant.
jokes.punchline: She asks for one big enough to be stuffed inside and smuggled out — Gary agrees without hesitation, as if this is a reasonable request — then adds 'a little raspberry surprise.'
jokes.explanation: A perfect closing beat. Selina's exhaustion rendered as a desire to be physically hidden inside a pastry is both absurdist and emotionally true. Gary's complete acceptance — 'Of course I can' — followed by 'a little raspberry surprise' is deeply Gary: he will do anything she asks, and he's already thought of an upgrade. The episode ends on Gary's unconditional, slightly unhinged devotion.

tmdb_overview: As a hostage crisis in Uzbekistan heats up, Selina and Secretary of Defense General Maddox have trouble getting on the same page at their joint appearance at the Marine Corps Base at Quantico. Sue testifies at a congressional hearing on governmental efficiencies; Dan and Gary jockey for the Veep's ear.
insight: Steady escalation carries a mid-tier episode through diplomatic hostage negotiations.
